articles.title: Watch The Reader’s Digest Escalator Interview with Author Mary H.K. Choi
articles.content:
玛丽·H·K·崔从不追求让自己的作品被整齐地归入某个类别。在凭借《紧急联系人》《永久记录》和《蛋黄》等畅销小说成为青少年文学领域备受喜爱的人物之后，她正携新作《泳池屋》迈入成人文学领域——不过，正如她所说，“我并不真正追求或致力于分类学。我不担心这本书会放在哪个书架上。我只是写自然而然出现的那本书。”

《泳池屋》讲述了一对失和的好莱坞搭档，他们住在自己的泳池屋里，而把主屋出租以维持经济收支。“这本书探讨的是权力和名望，以及两者不可避免的腐蚀和侵蚀，”她告诉《读者文摘》。这部小说已经因其对家庭关系的毫不留情的审视而获得赞誉，一些作家称其为“一段毫不留情的母女故事”。

对崔而言，为成年读者写作开启了一些新的可能。“这真的很有趣，”她说，“有时候当我谈论我的成人书时，听起来真的很低俗和色情。”但在表面的火花和尖锐边缘之下，是同样真挚的情感诚实，这正是多年来让读者与她的作品产生共鸣的原因。

除了畅销小说，崔还是一名记者，主持着关注心理健康和职业发展的播客节目《嘿，酷工作！》和《嘿，酷酷生活！》。她在香港和德克萨斯州长大，现居纽约市，围绕着提出深思熟虑的问题和讲述那些不回避生活更混乱角落的故事建立了自己的事业。

在《读者文摘》“扶梯访谈”系列的这一期中，我们在纽约市的巴诺书店与崔聊了聊《泳池屋》、她目前的阅读清单、她反复回归的舒适类型，以及她写作过程中令人惊讶的细节，还有更多内容。

观看独家采访视频，然后继续浏览她的书籍和阅读推荐！

在这篇文章中，《读者文摘》一百多年来一直在分享他们喜爱的书籍。他们曾与苏珊·奥尔良、珍妮特·伊万诺维奇和亚历克斯·哈里等畅销书作家合作，其中亚历克斯·哈里获得普利策奖的获奖作品《根》最初是由该杂志资助并首次发表的。通过“小说精选”系列，《读者文摘》数十年来一直在出版精选小说的合集。他们与詹姆斯·帕特森、露丝·威尔、克里斯汀·汉娜等小说界最知名的人物合作过。由图书编辑特蕾西·尼瑟科特主持的《读者文摘》读书俱乐部，为读者介绍更多当今最优秀的小说，涵盖新锐作家、畅销书作家和获奖作者。莱·贝尔兹·雷采访了本书作者玛丽·H·K·崔。
articles.summary:
Mary H.K. Choi has never been particularly interested in having her books fit neatly into a category. After becoming a beloved voice in young adult fiction with bestselling novels like Emergency Contact, Permanent Record and Yolk, she’s making the leap into adult fiction with Pool House—though, as she puts it, “I don’t really strive for or aim toward taxonomy. I don’t worry about what shelf the book is going to live on. I just write the book that arrives.”

In Pool House, a dysfunctional Hollywood duo finds themselves living in their own pool house while renting out their main home to stay afloat financially. “It’s about power and celebrity, and the corrosive effects of both and the erosion of both, which is inevitable,” she tells Reader’s Digest. The novel has already earned praise for its unflinching look at family dynamics, with some authors calling it an “unsparing mother-daughter story.”

For Choi, writing for an adult audience opened up a few new doors. “It was really fascinating,” she says. “Sometimes when I talk about my adult book, it sounds really tawdry and smutty.” But beneath the sparks and sharp edges is the same emotional honesty that has made readers connect with her work for years.

Beyond her bestselling novels, Choi is a journalist and the host of the mental-health and career-focused podcasts Hey, Cool Job! and Hey, Cool Life! Raised in Hong Kong and Texas and now based in New York City, she’s built a career around asking thoughtful questions and telling stories that don’t shy away from life’s messier corners.

For this episode of The Reader’s Digest Escalator Interview, we caught up with Choi at Barnes & Noble in New York City to talk about Pool House, what’s currently sitting in her reading pile, the comfort genres she returns to again and again, surprising details about her writing process and plenty more.

Watch the exclusive interview below, and then scroll down to shop her books and reading recommendations!

View this post on Instagram

A post shared by Reader’s Digest (@readersdigest)

Join the free Reader’s Digest Book Club for great reads, monthly discussions, author Q&As and a community of book lovers.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’ve been sharing our favorite books for over 100 years. We’ve worked with bestselling authors including Susan Orlean, Janet Evanovich and Alex Haley, whose Pulitzer Prize–winning Roots grew out of a project funded by and originally published in the magazine. Through Fiction Favorites (formerly Select Editions and Condensed Books), Reader’s Digest has been publishing anthologies of abridged novels for decades. We’ve worked with some of the biggest names in fiction, including James Patterson, Ruth Ware, Kristin Hannah and more. The Reader’s Digest Book Club, helmed by Books Editor Tracey Neithercott, introduces readers to even more of today’s best fiction by upcoming, bestselling and award-winning authors. For this piece, Leigh Belz Ray interviewed author Mary H.K. Choi. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

articles.title: Warning! If You Have a Dog, He Can Actually Start a Fire by Doing This
articles.content:
独自在家时，狗会惹上各种麻烦（我那副200美元的雷朋眼镜只存活了一天——现在我都在加油站买太阳镜了）。但即使是像我这样的半隐士，偶尔也必须出门。尽管我们拥有各种高科技设备，可以远程监视宠物、甚至给它们喂食，但我们无法时刻阻止它们搞破坏。

北卡罗来纳州教堂山的一户人家就亲历了这一幕。他们通过家庭安防摄像头惊恐地看到，自家宠物狗竟然在客厅地毯上玩起了火。虽然全家人都安然无恙，但情况本可能糟糕得多——实际上也经常如此。如果这种事能发生在他们身上，就可能发生在你身上。因此我咨询了理查德·梅尔（Richard Meier），他是梅尔火灾调查公司的首席专家，来了解狗为什么会以及如何引发家庭火灾。

请继续阅读——拥有超过15年经验和700多起火灾及爆炸调查经历的梅尔，将为我们详细解读这个日益常见却令人担忧的现象。

订阅Reader's Digest的Read Up新闻通讯，每周获取更多安全知识、幽默故事、旅行趣闻、科技动态和趣味科普。

引发火灾的方式有很多！但在这里，我们讨论的是咀嚼含有锂离子电池设备的狗。那些导致汽车和飞机发生毁灭性火灾的电池，同样会危及我们的家庭。“任何含有锂离子电池的设备都可能发生故障，”梅尔说，“幸运的是，大多数故障是良性的，但当它们发生灾难性故障时，情况会非常严重。”

美国国家消防协会（NFPA）引用过一个常被提及但较早的统计数据：宠物每年大约引发1000起家庭火灾，尽管目前的数字可能更高。目前还没有关于宠物引发锂离子火灾的具体数据，但狗（和猫）确实会意外打开炉灶燃烧器——就像今年3月在缅因州发生的那样，它们会打翻蜡烛，还会像教堂山那家人发现的那样，咀嚼含有锂离子电池的设备。

这一切都源于一种叫做“热失控”的现象，即电池进入一种无法控制的自我加热状态。这可能是由撞击造成的，也可能是充电或放电过程中内部故障引起的。

“所以说，当狗咀嚼玩具时，会发生的情况是：它们会刺穿电池，或者仅仅是咬合的力量就会对电池造成内部损伤，导致阳极和阴极之间发生短路，”梅尔说，“通常遵循相同的过程：电池变得越来越热，热量无法散发出去。”

这就导致了我们在那段可怕的监控视频中看到的场景。“越来越多的内部故障发生，最终会引发火灾或爆炸，”梅尔说。更糟糕的是，锂离子火灾极难扑灭。他说，对于较大的火灾（如汽车电池），消防员可能需要使用“成千上万加仑的水”。

“哦，这种情况正在变得非常普遍，”梅尔说，“我手边的电脑鼠标里就有锂离子电池，”他补充道，然后列举了一长串潜在的“罪魁祸首”，包括手机和遥控器。不知道你们怎么样，但我把鼠标放在紧挨着我的沙发旁边（就在我家狗的咀嚼范围之内），也许我应该重新考虑一下这个问题。

以下是其他一些含有锂离子电池的常见物品：

“一般来说，电池越大，问题就越严重，”梅尔说，“并不是说小电池就不会发生故障，但它们发生故障时通常更为良性。”但请记住，任何故障都有可能点燃附近的物品（如地毯、窗帘和床上用品）。

电线是一个重大隐患，作为一名电工，我完全认同这一观点（这是NFPA密切关注的一个数据）。据NFPA报告，2020年至2024年间，美国每年有超过46000起家庭建筑火灾是由电气故障或故障（包括电线和电源线）引起的。

虽然狗在这里背负了坏名声，但梅尔说我们不应该忘记猫。“我以前养过一只猫，我不知道它怎么没有被电死，因为它特别喜欢咬电线，”他说。所以无论你养什么宠物，保持警惕都是值得的。电气火灾每年占所有家庭建筑火灾的13%，占所有家庭建筑火灾死亡人数的18%。

梅尔说，最明智的做法就是收拾好你的东西，不要把东西乱放，无论你是在家还是出门。这些火灾发生得很快！“把（设备）放在宠物够不到的地方，在家时也要留意你的宠物。”他还说，不要在沙发或床上给这些设备充电，因为不管有没有狗的咀嚼，这都是火灾隐患。

他还特别提醒，扔东西到垃圾桶时要非常小心。任何含有锂离子电池的物品都需要送到适当的回收机构处理，因为它们每年会在垃圾处理设施引发数百起火灾。“这类火灾的报道不多，但对垃圾处理设施来说是一个持续性的危险。”

关于专家

在Reader's Digest，我们致力于生产高质量内容，由具有专业知识和经验的 writers 撰写，并咨询相关合格的专家。我们依靠政府、专业组织和学术机构等可靠的主要来源，以及作者在适当情况下的个人经历。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可靠来源支持，并在后续持续跟进以确保其准确性和时效性。了解更多关于我们团队、贡献者和编辑政策的信息。

这篇文章《警告！如果你养狗，它实际上可以通过做这件事引发火灾》首次发表于Reader's Digest。
articles.summary:
Dogs get into all sorts of trouble when left alone (my $200 Ray-Bans lasted one day—now I buy sunglasses at the gas station). But even part-time hermits like me have to leave the house occasionally, and despite all of our high-tech devices, which allow us to watch our pets and even feed them from afar, we can’t always keep them from wreaking havoc.

That’s exactly what happened to a family in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, who watched in horror (via their home security camera) as their pet pooch started a fire right on their living room rug. Everyone was OK, but it could have been much worse—and often is. If it can happen to them, it can happen to you, so I consulted Richard Meier, the principal expert at Meier Fire Investigation, to learn how and why dogs can start home fires.

Read on as Meier, who has more than 15 years and 700 fire and explosion investigations under his belt, walks us through this scary but increasingly common situation.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more safety, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

How can a dog start a fire when left home alone?

So many ways! But here, we’re talking about dogs who chew on devices containing lithium-ion batteries. Yes, those same batteries that cause devastating car and plane fires can affect our homes too. “Anything with a lithium-ion battery can fail,” Meier says. “Fortunately, most of them fail in a benign fashion, but when they fail catastrophically, it can be very serious.”

An oft-cited, yet older, statistic from the National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) is that pets cause about 1,000 home fires a year, although the current number is likely higher. There’s no specific data yet about pets causing lithium fires, but dogs (and cats) can and do accidentally turn on stove burners, as happened in Maine in March of this year, knock over candles and, as the Chapel Hill family discovered, chew on devices containing lithium-ion batteries.

Why do lithium batteries burst into flames like this?

It’s due to something called thermal runaway, which happens when the battery enters a stage of uncontrolled self-heating. This can be caused by trauma or an internal failure during charging or discharging.

“So, in the case of a dog chewing on a toy, what’s going to happen is that they will puncture the battery, or just the force of the bite will cause some internal damage to the battery, which will cause an electrical short between the anode and the cathode,” Meier says. “It generally follows the same course: It gets hotter and hotter, and it can’t dissipate the heat.”

And that leads to what we saw on the scary security video. “More and more failures occur internally, and then it ends up being a fire or an explosion,” Meier says. What makes things worse is that lithium fires are extremely hard to put out. He says that for larger fires (like car batteries), firefighters can end up using “thousands and thousands of gallons of water.”

What else are lithium batteries in that your dog can get his paws (and mouth) on?

“Oh, it’s becoming very ubiquitous,” Meier says. “The mouse on my computer right now has a lithium-ion battery in it,” he added, before rattling off a long list of potential culprits, including cellphones and remote controls. I don’t know about you, but I keep my mouse on the couch right next to me (and my dog’s gnawing teeth), so maybe I should rethink that.

Here are some other common items that contain lithium-ion batteries:

  • Radio-controlled toys
  • Smart watches
  • Fitness trackers
  • Earbuds
  • Flashlights
  • Television remote controls
  • Rechargeable vape devices

“Generally speaking, the bigger the battery, the bigger the problem,” Meier says. “Not that the little ones can’t fail, but they’re generally more benign when they do.” But remember, any failure has the potential to ignite materials (like rugs, curtains and bedding) in the vicinity.

What other items can cause a fire if a dog chews on them?

Electrical wires are a big one, and as an electrician, I concur (that’s a stat the NFPA tracks religiously). Between 2020 and 2024, the NFPA reported more than 46,000 annual home structure fires in the United States that were caused by electrical failure or malfunction, including electrical wires and cords.

But while dogs are getting a bad rap here, Meier says we shouldn’t forget about cats. “I had one cat, and I don’t know how he didn’t electrocute himself, because he liked to chew on electrical cords a lot,” he says. So whatever pet you have, it pays to be vigilant. Electrical fires cause 13% of all home structure fires and 18% of all home structure fires deaths each year.

What should you do so this doesn’t happen?

Meier says the smartest thing to do is just pick up your stuff and don’t leave things lying around, whether you’re at home or away. These fires happen fast! “Put [devices] in a place where pets can’t get to them, and keep an eye on your pets when you’re home.” He also says not to charge these devices on the couch or bed, because regardless of Fido’s chewing, it’s a fire danger.

He also says to be very careful about throwing things in the garbage. Anything with a lithium-ion battery needs to be taken to an appropriate recycling facility because they cause hundreds of trash fires every year, particularly at waste facilities. “They don’t get a lot of reporting, but it’s a constant danger for them.”

RELATED:

  • American Airlines Just Changed Its Portable Charger Policy, and You’re Going to Want to Read This—It’s a Matter of Safety
  • The Horrifying Mistake You’re Probably Making with Your Dog’s Food Bowl—And What to Do Instead
  • Meet Penny, the Pup That Won Best in Show at the Westminster Kennel Club Dog Show (You’re Totally Going to Fall in Love!)

About the expert

  • Richard Meier is the principal expert at Meier Fire Investigation, which operates in Florida and nine other states. After working as a mechanical engineer for nearly 25 years, Meier became a fire and arson investigator. He is a Certified Fire Investigator (IAAI-CFI), Certified Fire & Explosion Investigator (CFEI) and Certified Vehicle Fire Investigator, and he has overseen more than 700 fire and explosion investigations nationwide.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • Richard Meier, principal expert at Meier Fire Investigation; phone interview, June 5, 2026
  • WRAL News: “Dog sparks Chapel Hill house fire from chewing on lithium-ion battery device”
  • WMTW 8: “Dogs likely started Belgrade house fire that caused their deaths, Maine investigators say”
  • NFPA: “Electrical Safety in the Home”
  • American Red Cross: “Pet Fire Safety”
